coredata.dc:title: Structural features regulated photoluminescence intensity and cell internalization of carbon and graphene quantum dots for bioimaging
coredata.dc:creator: Choppadandi, Mounika
Guduru, Aditya Teja
Gondaliya, Piyush
Arya, Neha
Kalia, Kiran
Kumar, Hemant
Kapusetti, Govinda
coredata.dc:description: Carbon-based nanostructures with nanometer dimensions have been identified as potential photoluminescence probes for bioimaging due to their biocompatibility, tunable bandgap, and resistance to photobleaching. However, the influence of structural features of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) in bioimaging has not been explored previously. In the present investigation, we elucidated the mechanism of higher PL in GQDs as compared to CQDs as a function of their structural features. TEM and AFM studies revealed that CQDs were spherical (size ~5 nm), while GQDs showed zigzag edges (size ~3 nm). Further, XRD and NMR studies confirmed that CQDs and GQDs show amorphous and crystalline structures with greater sp 2 clusters, respectively. While both the QDs demonstrated multicolor fluorescence against variable excitations with similar lifetime, GQDs showed 7-fold higher QY than CQDs. Bioimaging studies in 2D cell culture, 3D tumoroids, and in vivo suggested a greater intensity of fluorescence in GQDs than CQDs. Additionally, rapid cell internalization was observed in GQDs owing to their positive surface potential by heterogeneous atomic (N and S) doping. Moreover, both CQDs and GQDs have demonstrated better time dependent stability for fluorescence properties. Taken together, the proposed mechanism elucidates the greater PL intensity in GQDs due to quantum confinement effect, crystallinity, and surface edge effects and is a better candidate for bioimaging amongst the carbon family.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structural characterization of CQDs and GQDs. (a and c) TEM micrograms of CQDs and GQDs at 10 nm scale (insert showing size distribution curves with scale 1 nm), (b) The broad XRD pattern of CQDs reveals the amorphous nature, (d) XRD pattern of GQDs suggesting the crystallinity with an interlayer spacing of 0.25 nm (002), (e and f) Atomic force micrographs (AFM) micrograms representing the height and roughness of CQDs with 5–6 nm, while QDs shows 1.5–2.5 nm (g and h).

Functional groups identification of CQDs and GQDs. (a) IR spectra of CQDs along with precursor material gelatin, (b) IR spectra of GQDs along with precursor materials citric acid and thiourea, (c) XPS spectrum of CQDs, (d) XPS spectrum of GQDs, (e) the high-resolution C1s peaks and fitting curves of CQDs, and (f) the high-resolution C1s peaks and fitting curves of GQDs.

(a and c) UV–Visible spectra of CQDs and GQDs, respectively, and the inserted digital images show the fluorescence under long UV excitation (greenish-blue and blue). (b and d) Multi-colored fluorescence spectra with variable excitations of CQDs and GQDs, respectively, CQDs show emission maximum at 490 nm at excitation of 420 nm, while GQDs show maximum emission at 450 nm the excitation of 370 nm, and (e) Fluorescence decay curve of CQDs and GQDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Cytotoxicity study in MCF-7 cell line using MTT assay, (a and b) cells treated with CQDs and GQDs with different concentrations (20, 40. 60, 80, 100, and 120 μg/ml), respectively, and (c) Schematic representation of QDs internalization into cells based on size and charge-dependent pinocytosis.

Intracellular localization of CQDs, GQDs, and DAPI in MCF-7 cells. (a) MCF-7 cell line incubated with CQDs, GQDs, and DAPI at excitation wavelengths 405, 485, and 543 nm (scale bar: 25 μm), and (b) Normalized fluorescence intensity at different excitation wavelength with various incubation time points.
CLSM imaging of MCF-7 cells treated with CQDs and GQDs depicting clear localization of CQDs and GQDs in cytosol and nucleus at different time points (scale bar: 25 μm).

Bioimaging in 3D tumoroids and in vivo models, (a) MCF-7 tumoroid imaging at various excitation energies (405, 485, and 543 nm) following incubation with CQDs, GQDs and DAPI (scale bar – 100 μm), and (b) Fluorescence imaging in mice using CQDs and GQDs with IVIS spectrum imaging system.
Illustration depicting the fluorescence intensity in CQDs and GQDs with the different exciton recombination.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon-based nanostructures with nanometer dimensions have been identified as potential photoluminescence probes for bioimaging due to their biocompatibility, tunable bandgap, and resistance to photobleaching. However, the influence of structural features of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) in bioimaging has not been explored previously. In the present investigation, we elucidated the mechanism of higher PL in GQDs as compared to CQDs as a function of their structural features. TEM and AFM studies revealed that CQDs were spherical (size ~5 nm), while GQDs showed zigzag edges (size ~3 nm). Further, XRD and NMR studies confirmed that CQDs and GQDs show amorphous and crystalline structures with greater sp 2 clusters, respectively. While both the QDs demonstrated multicolor fluorescence against variable excitations with similar lifetime, GQDs showed 7-fold higher QY than CQDs. Bioimaging studies in 2D cell culture, 3D tumoroids, and in vivo suggested a greater intensity of fluorescence in GQDs than CQDs. Additionally, rapid cell internalization was observed in GQDs owing to their positive surface potential by heterogeneous atomic (N and S) doping. Moreover, both CQDs and GQDs have demonstrated better time dependent stability for fluorescence properties. Taken together, the proposed mechanism elucidates the greater PL intensity in GQDs due to quantum confinement effect, crystallinity, and surface edge effects and is a better candidate for bioimaging amongst the carbon family.

ce:section.ce:para: Quantum dots (QDs) are nanocrystalline semiconductor nanoparticles with a size less than or equal to the exciton Bohr radius [1–4]. Tiny particles of size <10 nm hold a unique feature called the quantum confinement effect and show properties similar to atoms. In recent years, they have attracted extensive attention due to their exceptional chemical stability, photoluminescence (PL), electrical conductivity, and a wide range of optical absorption [5]. As a result, QDs have been extensively explored in numerous applications such as photocatalysis, bioimaging, sensors, and electrochemical luminescence [6,7] The high-performance optical characteristics of QDs produce exciting results in a variety of biological applications over organic dyes due to their PL and photo-stability [8,9]. However, the apparent advantages of QDs are highly challenged by some serious concerns such as toxicity and solubility from the major players of the QD family i.e. cadmium, gallium, and zinc [10,11]. Interestingly, with the discovery of carbon-based PL QDs, carbon has evolved as an effective alternative to semiconductor QDs [12,13].

Towards this, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have emerged as superior PL probes for various biomedical applications (such as bioimaging and optical sensing) [14] due to their key advantages, including better biocompatibility, strong photostability, tuneable PL, exceptional multi-photon excitation characteristics, electrochemiluminescence, magnetic relaxivity, excellent chemical inertness, ease of production and bioconjugation [15,16].

The new family of the zero-dimensional carbon QDs comprises sp 2 carbon clusters and hybridized with abundant oxygenous residues (sp 3). The distinct structures were predominant in CQDs and GQDs. The CQDs are quasi-spherical amorphous nanoparticles first reported by Sun et al. in the year 2006 [12]. Some reports suggested the existence of diamond-like sp 3 carbons, even with hollow-structured particles [17–20]. In contrast, GQDs are single, or a few layered crystalline structures with an average lattice parameter of 0.24 nm, and they were first reported by Pan et al. in 2010 [21].

Interestingly, the GQDs are isolated aromatic structures, some are carbines at zigzag sites; derived suitable bandgaps and radiated exciton recombination could generate strong PL [22,23]. The exact PL mechanism in CQDs and GQDs is not yet well understood. Usually, the CQDs are less bright than GQDs due to low QY (< 10%) restricted from its deep traps on the surface, while the strong PL in GQDs can be attributed to their layered structure and better crystallinity [15,24]. Indeed, there is no perfect practical explanation for PL in CQDs and GQDs, but it is explained with density-functional theory (DFT) models elsewhere [7,25]. The theories suggested that the PL in CQDs is primarily from their surface properties, with less participation of sp 2 clusters (crystallinity) and quantum confinement effect. Overall, the PL of CQDs could be determined from surface emissive traps, and it is restricted mainly by non-radiated recombination. The brighter PL in GQDs is reported from their stronger quantum-confined π- electrons in sp 2 clusters of crystalline carbon network, edge configuration (zigzag/armchair), the shape of the particle, conjugated functional groups, doping, and defects [26].

Further, the doping of heterogeneous atoms could influence the cell internalization characteristic of GQDs by modulating the surface charge. Flak et al. synthesized positively charged graphene quantum dots – mesoporous silica nanoparticles composite (GQD-MSNs) complex which effectively internalized cell via electrostatic cellular uptake mechanism due to the cell's negative surface potential [27,28]. However, the mechanism of CQDs and GQDs uptake in cancer cells as a function of their structural properties has not been elucidated yet.

In this study, we synthesized CQDs and GQDs via a one-pot hydrothermal method. The synthesized QDs were characterized by TEM and AFM for topographical and structural analysis. The XRD and NMR studies were carried out to understand the carbon backbone's crystal structure and hybridization. Further, multicolor emission of QDs and quantum yield (QY) were analyzed from fluorescence spectra. Two different cell lines (MCF-7 and MDA-MD-231) are subjected to PL imaging following incubation with CQDs and GQDs. The results were further validated using 3D tumoroids and in vivo studies. This is the first study that reports the disparity in properties and PL of CQDs and GQDs based on the current results and available computational models.

Gelatin (extra pure) was procured from Himedia (India), citric acid purchased from SDFCL (India), and thiourea was acquired from Sigma Aldrich (USA). Cell culture materials Dulbecco's modified Eagle's media (DMEM), L15 media, fetal bovine serum (FBS), phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, pH 7.4), trypsin-EDTA solution (0.25% trypsin and 0.02% EDTA in Dulbecco's PBS without phenol red), 3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) dye and antibiotic penstrep were purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific (USA). Distilled water was used throughout all experimental studies.

The CQDs were synthesized by the hydrothermal method using gelatin as a precursor. The gelatin (0.8 g) was dissolved in 40 ml of water at 40 °C under gentle agitation. The reaction mixture was then transferred into a teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 3 h. After cooling to room temperature, the collected yellowish solution was centrifuged to remove the aggregated particles. The light brown aqueous solution was freeze-dried and used for further characterizations [29]. Similarly, the GQDs were synthesized from citric acid and thiourea. Briefly, 0.84 g of citric acid and 0.92 g of thiourea dissolved in 20 ml water. Then the reaction mixture was heated at 160 °C for 4 h in a teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. After cooling to room temperature, GQDs were obtained by adding the ethanol into the solution during centrifugation at 5000 rpm for 5 min. After centrifugation, the supernatant was collected and lyophilized for one week directly without any processing. Then the freeze-dried powder was collected and stored in dark at 4 °C until further use [30–33].

The morphological analysis of the CQDs and GQDs was investigated by using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The high-resolution TEM images were taken on ultra-thin carbon-coated copper grids on FEI, Themis 60-300. Similarly, the atomic force microscopy (AFM) analysis of the CQDs and GQDs was carried out by Multimode 8, Bruker, USA AFM. CQDs and GQDs were immobilized on the glass side and analyzed; the data acquisition was carried out by Nanoscope software. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy analysis was performed by using FTIR A1, Bruker, Germany, at a resolution of 16 cm−1 with scanning wavelength ranges from 400 to 4000 cm 1. The crystal structure and crystallinity of the CQDs and GQDs were examined by the X-ray diffraction (XRD) (D8 DISCOVER Bruker) using Ni-filtered CuKα1 radiation. The scanning was performed from 2θ = 20 to 50° at 10 kV. The UV–Visible spectroscopy of CQDs and GQDs was recorded using a Thermo Scientific UV–Visible spectrophotometer in the wavelength range of 200–800 nm. The fluorescence spectroscopic analysis was carried out with excitation wavelength 420–500 nm and 350–440 nm for CQDs and GQDs, respectively, and fluorescence lifetime decay curve measured by time-correlated single-photon counting method (TCSPC) by using Fluorolog-3, Horiba fluorescence spectrophotometer. Stability of CQDs and GQDs was performed with respect to time till 4 weeks. The fluorescence intensity, QY, lifetime, and absorbance of CQDs and GQDs were determined in each week to determine the stability. Anti-interference study was performed in the presence of various ions present in simulated body fluid (SBF) (Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Cl−, HCO3−, HPO4 2−, and SO4 2− with concentration 142.0, 5.0, 1.5, 2.5, 148.8, 4.2, 1.0, and 0.5 mM, respectively). The fluorescence intensity of QDs was measured in SBF using fluorescence spectroscopy. The elemental analysis and the chemical composition of the CQDs and GQDs were analyzed by energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX). EDX mapping was acquired using EDAX Ametek, USA. The binding energies and elemental composition were analyzed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), K-Alpha XPS system, Thermo Fisher Scientific Pvt. Ltd. UK. The QY of the CQDs and GQDs was analyzed by employing a previously established protocol [34–36] (supplementary data).

The cell viability of the CQDs and GQDs was determined by using MCF-7 cell line with a cell density of 1 × 10−5/well in a 96 well plate. The cells were treated with different concentrations of CQDs and GQDs; 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, and 120 μg/ml for 24 h in 5% CO2 environment at 37 °C. Upon compilation of the incubation of 20 μl of MTT (methyl thiazole tetrazolium) reagent (5 mg/ml) was added in each well and incubated for 4 h. Afterward, the media was discarded, and formazan crystal was dissolved in DMSO then the absorbance was recorded at 570 nm using a UV-plate reader (Multiskan™ GO, Thermo Scientific, Finland) [37,38]. The study was carried out in triplicate. The percentage of cell viability was calculated using the following formula:
Statistical analysis was carried out by employing one-way ANOVA followed by Dunnett's Multiple Comparison Test, (P < 0.05), n = 6.

The fluorescent cell imaging of CQDs and GQDs was carried out in MCF-7 (hormone-dependent breast cancer cell line) and MDA-MB-231 (triple-negative cell line) by using confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM), Leica, TCS SP 8. DAPI treated cells were taken as a positive control against QDs. Initially, the cells (MCF-7 and MDA-MB-231) were seeded on a coverslip then placed in a 6 well plate. Further, they incubated at 37 °C under 5% CO2 and 95% air atmosphere for 24 h. After incubation, the culture medium was replaced with CQDs, GQDs, and DAPI (positive control) and incubated for different time intervals (2, 4, 6, and 8 h). Prior to imaging, the cells were washed with PBS [39,40].

The 3D tumoroid model was generated using the MCF-7 cell line (P-7-P9) with 3% methylcellulose as previously developed in our laboratory [41]. Briefly, 10,000 cells/2 μl drop were injected in methylcellulose. Prior to use, the culture dishes were coated with Polyhydroxyethylmethacrylate (PolyHEMA). After 3 days of culture, the tumoroids were retrieved in PBS and transferred to a 12 well plate coated with 1% agarose for the treatment with CQDs and GQDs. After incubation of tumoroids with QDs for 6 and 24 h, they were washed with PBS, fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde (PFA), and imaged using a confocal microscope at 405, 485, and 543 nm filters. For positive control, tumoroids were stained with DAPI [38].

The mouse (C57BL/6) model was adopted for the in vivo fluorescence imagine of CQDs and GQDs. Prior to the study, the animals were housed in a facility with 55–65% humidity at 24 ± 2 °C with a 12 h light/dark cycle and food and water were available ad libitum. All animal experiments were performed as per the approved guidelines by the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee (IAEC) of NIPER-A (NIPERA/IAEC/2020/033) and principles of laboratory animal care. Animals were anesthetized with a combination of ketamine and xylazine. Briefly, animals were intravenously injected with CQDs and GQDs to confirm the fluorescence using in vivo spectrum imaging system (IVIS).

Following the synthesis of CQDs and GQDs, they were subjected to morphological characterization using TEM. Fig. 1 a and c show the TEM images of CQDs and GQDs, respectively, along with their size distribution curves. The CQDs demonstrated a perfect spherical shape with an average diameter of around 5 nm (Fig. 1a). Further, TEM analysis revealed CQDs as a solid spear structure without any bright centres. Hence, CQD may offer PL by quantum confinement effect [17]. On the other hand, GQDs appeared as zigzag edged particles with a mean diameter of 3 ± 0.02 nm (Fig. 1c). The image clearly showed the layered structure of GQDs with an interlayer distance of 0.25 nm, which indicates (100) the graphene lattice orientation [42]. Further, the 3D AFM micrograms revealed the spherical and layered topography for CQDs (Fig. 1e and f) and GQDs (Fig. 1g and h), respectively. The observed size of the CQDs and GQDs was ~5 and 2 nm, respectively, suggesting that the GQDs comprised more than one layer (Fig. 1g and h).

The presence of functional groups on CQDs and GQDs was examined by using FT-IR analysis. Fig. 2 a illustrates the transmission spectra of CQDs along with their precursors. A broad peak was observed at 3500 cm−1, indicating hydroxyl groups (-OH). The absorption band at 1700 cm−1 was assigned to the stretching vibration band of CO. The transmission bands at 2950, 1420, and 1330 cm−1 indicated the stretching vibrations of CH, CC, and CC. The spectra of gelatin was similar to the reported literature [29] (Fig. 2a). The FT-IR spectra of GQDs revealed a broad transmission peak at 3000–3500 cm−1 attributed to the stretching vibration of OH and NH. A small peak at 1709 cm−1 indicated the stretching vibration of CO. The bending vibration CN was peak appeared at 1401 cm−1. The characteristic peak at 1083 cm−1 is attributed to the presence of CS stretching (Fig. 2b). The spectra confirm the presence of oxygenated functional groups and N, S doping in the graphene backbone. The transmission spectra of the precursors also matched with reported literature [30].

The structural information of CQDs and GQDs was analyzed using XRD analysis and profiles presented in Fig. 1b and d. A broad reflection was observed at around 2θ = 20°, ascribing the low crystallinity for graphitic structure with the interlayer spacing of 0.33 nm (002) [43]. Further, the amorphous nature of CQDs is explained with the synthesis procedure. Plausibly, the gelatin was pyrolyzed to small molecules during the hydrothermal process; upon the hydrothermal treatment, it was pyrolyzed into amino acids with abundant amino and carboxy groups. With continued thermal processing, the CQDs are functionalized with oxygen and amino groups by subsequent decomposition and polymerization of amino acids followed by carbonization at CQDs defect sites (Fig. 1b) [29]. This results in the reduction of graphitic structure by converting sp 2 hybridization clusters into sp 3. Fig. 1d shows the XRD pattern of GQDs attributed to the poor crystallinity as compared to the graphite. The diffraction peak at 2θ = 22° represents the (002) plane with interlayer distance d = 0.24 nm [44]. The reduced d-spacing in GQDs against graphite (0.33 nm) suggests the presence of oxygenated groups. Hence, the GQDs exhibited greater crystallinity than CQDs due to the presence of layered graphene clusters.

Apart from XRD analysis, nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) is widely used to understand the structural insights of carbon frameworks by analyzing the hybridization in the crystalline lattices of carbon atoms [45]. The S.Fig. 5a and c show the 1H NMR of CQDs and GQDs, respectively. The presence of 1H is quite evident in both QDs thereby revealing the presence of sp 3 clusters. However, CQDs showed various sp 3 clusters in large numbers than GQDs, suggesting more protons (S.Fig. 5a). The 13C NMR could differentiate sp 2 and sp 3 hybridized carbons (S.Fig. 5b). Plausibly, the layered GQDs have a graphite-like structure with z-stacking of sp 2 hybridization, and the functional groups are primarily conjugated at edges. While in CQDs, the functional groups primarily occupied the surface defects and demonstrated more sp 3 clusters than sp 2 (S.Fig. 5c and d). Therefore, it is understood that the GQDs have more sp 2 clusters alike graphite than the CQDs, and this observation is quite supportive of the XRD analysis [46,47].

The chemical composition of CQDs and GQDs was determined by using the XPS technique. Fig. 2c and d show the XPS survey spectrum of CQDs and GQDs, respectively. The spectrum of CQDs displayed three prominent peaks at 285, 400, and 531 eV which are attributed to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively, suggesting the presence of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen. The C1s spectrum exhibited four peaks at 284.2, 285.5, 287.2, and 288.2 eV, which represents the CC, CN, CO, and OC groups, portraying the presence of different functionalities in CQDs (Fig. 2e) [29,48,49]. While, the survey spectrum of GQDs illustrates four peaks at 164, 286, 401, and 532 eV resembling the binding energies of S2p, C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively. The result suggests the doping of S and N in GQDs via a one-pot hydrothermal synthesis route. Meanwhile, the high-resolution C1s spectra exhibit four distinct peaks (Fig. 2f) at 284.4, 285.8, 287.7, and 288.1 eV endorsed the presence of C=C/C ̶ C in the aromatic region of the carbon core, C ̶ OH/C ̶ O/C ̶ N, CO, and O ̶ CO determined the presence of surface functional groups and dopants [50–52].

Further, the EDX study was carried to identify the percentage of elements in CQDs and GQDs (S.Fig. 6a). It was noted that CQDs have a C, N, O, and S with atomic percentages of 58.2, 15.5, 19.8, and 6.4, respectively. While, GQDs are composed of C, N, O, and S with atomic percentages of 23.2, 19.4, 26.1, and 31.3, respectively. The results are suggesting that CQDs have more carbon, while GQDs are with functional groups and dopants (S.Fig. 6b).

The absorption spectrum of CQDs and GQDs was obtained from UV–Visible spectrophotometer and depicted in Fig. 3 a and c, respectively. The QDs exhibited stronger adsorption in the UV region and an extended lower absorbance tail towards the visible region. CQDs exhibited a broad peak around 250–300 nm contributing to the n-p* transition of carbonyl group on the aromatic backbone of carbon (Fig. 3a). On the other hand, GQDs showed absorption maxima at 345 and 595 nm due to doping with sulfur. The band at 595 nm is attributed to the π → π*, and n → π* of CS and SO (Fig. 3c). The inserted optical images show the greenish-blue and blue fluorescence under long UV exposure in aqueous solutions of CQDs and GQDs, respectively, revealed the existence of PL [29,30].

Fig. 3b and d show the PL spectrograms of CQDs and GQDs, respectively, with red-shifted emissions against a bunch of excitations with a wavelength from low to high. Both CQDs and GQDs demonstrated excitation dependent multi emission characteristics. However, an emission maximum was observed around 490 nm in CQDs with an excitation of 430 nm (Fig. 3b). While GQDs showed maximum emission at 450 nm at the excitation of 370 nm (Fig. 3d). Hence, the QDs can able to show multi-colored emissions with variable excitations. This PL effect was firmly observed in PL carbon nanomaterials possibly due to the presence of a variety of surface defects, which offers a wide range of optical selections [13]. When the excitation wavelength is shifting towards 400 nm, weak fluorescence was evident due to sulfur doping in GQDs.

The calculated QY of CQDs and GQDs was 6.2 and 44.1%, respectively. Interestingly, GQDs exhibited significantly high QY against CQDs. Generally, the QY of CQDs is <10% and restricted them to use in sensing and cell imaging applications. Possibly, the excitation-dependent PL of CQDs is no longer evident at higher wavelengths due to immobilization of CQDs, suggesting that PL is originated from deep edge defects or surface traps instead of the quantum confinement effect [53]. While the strong QY in GQDs is due to the layered structure, higher crystallinity, and doping (S and N atoms) [54,55].

The PL decay lifetime measures the time QDs spend in an excited state before coming to the ground state. Fig. 3e shows the time decay curves of CQDs and GQDs, and the lifetime was obtained at the time when 37% of the population at an excited state by using TCSPC method [56]. A prolonged lifetime is inevitable for various biomedical applications, primarily for cell imaging. CQDs and GQDs demonstrated a lifetime of 7.6 and 8.2 ns, respectively and the values are very similar to previously reported literature with higher lifetime values as compared to the conventional dyes (Fig. 3e) [30,57].

The stability of QDs was evaluated through fluorescence spectroscopy, UV–Visible spectroscopy, QY, and lifetime, the results are presented in S.Fig. 1a and b, S.Fig. 2a and b, Table S1, and S.Fig. 3a and b, respectively. The fluorescence emission maximum was remained intact with time-dependent analysis (0, 7, 14, 21, and 28 days) in both CQDs and GQDs. However, the slight fluorescence quenching was observed in both the CQDs, possibly the development of small heterogeneity by agglomeration of QDs [40]. A similar, trend was observed in UV–Visible spectroscopy, where the λmax is intact with a slight reduction in absorbance. The QY was measured in both CQDs and GQDs with different time intervals. The QY was slightly reduced in both CQDs and GQDs but no significant reduction was evident till 2 weeks (14 days), suggesting the high stability of QDs. Further, the lifetime measurement also depicts the same trend as QY with a slight decrease in intensity. Therefore, the QDs were demonstrated good stability due to their surface charge and structural features [58]. The anti-interference performance of QDs was measured in SBF, it has an ion composition similar to human body fluids (Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Cl−, HCO3−, HPO4 2−, and SO4 2−). The fluorescence emission of QDs in SBF is similar to QDs dispersion in water as there is no fluorescent quenching in CQDs and GQDs (S.Fig. 4). Previous reports are suggesting that the GQDs have shown similar fluorescence with different ions types and biomolecules with high concentrations [14,31–33,40]. Therefore, QDs can give stable fluorescence even in a biological environment and they can be a better molecule for bioimaging.

Even though semiconductor QDs exhibited better PL, their application is confined in bioimaging due to their toxicity. Towards this, biocompatible carbon-nanostructures are well accepted for biomedical applications when used within permissible limits [59–62]. Therefore, in this study we evaluated CQDs, and GQDs for biocompatibility 3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay to identify their permeable concentration limit. For this, breast cancer cells (MCF-7 and MBA-MD-321) were exposed to different concentrations of CQDs and GQDs (20, 40, 60, 80,100 and 120 μg/ml) for 24 h and untreated cells were considered as control. Fig. 4 a and b show the percentage of cell viability of CQD and GQDs, respectively. The observed cell viability was >80% in MCF-7 even in high concentrations (120 μg/ml); a parallel trend was observed in MDA MB-321 cells as well, suggesting that both the QDs are biocompatible and results are similar to the previously published reports [63,64] (Fig. 4a and b).

The potential of CQDs and GQDs as PL probes was tested by incubating breast cancer cells (MCF-7 and MDA MB-231) with QDs. The cells were incubated with QDs at a 100 μg/ml concentration for 2, 4, 6, and 8 h, which was proved as a safe dose through cytotoxicity assay (Fig. 4a and b). DAPI was taken as a reference standard for the evolution of fluorescence efficiency, and the imaging was carried out by using CLSM. Fig. 5 a shows the fluorescence images of MCF-7 cells treated with DAPI, CQDs, and GQDs. Interestingly, multicolor images were observed against different excitation wavelengths in QDs, particularly blue, green, and red fluorescence was evident from the excitation of 405, 485, and 543 nm, respectively. To add to this, the intense blue and green fluorescence was evident in GQDs, and stronger red fluorescence was observed in CQDs. A similar, trend was observed in MDA-MB-231 cells, and results are presented in S.Fig. 8. DAPI showed only blue fluorescence at 405 nm excitation and fluorescence was absent in remaining lasers (Fig. 5a).

Further, fluorescence intensity was quantified by using ImageJ software, and results (Fig. 5b) suggested that GQDs demonstrated stronger fluorescence than CQDs at both excitations (405 and 485 nm). Interestingly, the intensity was higher than the DAPI. The study was conducted up to 8 h of an interval of 2 h. The QDs were shown constant intensity after 4 h of incubation, while in DAPI a showed decreased trend after 4 h of incubation. Hence, the results suggested that the QDs were proved to have stronger photobleaching resistance against the conventional dye (DAPI) [65–69].

Furthermore, the QDs were observed mostly in the cytoplasm of MCF-7 cells at the end of 2 h and later moved into the nucleus. The inserted images (merged image of bright field and fluorescence) demonstrated the localization in the cytoplasm. With the increase in incubation period (Fig. 6 ), the fluorescence intensity was more prominent in the nucleus, with high intensity in GQDs as compared to CQDs at all time points (2, 4, 6, and 8 h). Higher fluorescence intensity in GQDs suggests their rapid internalization as compared to the CQDs. Generally, the cell internalization of nanoparticles primarily relies on the size; diameter < 200 nm involves clathrin-coated pits, and 200–500 nm follows the caveolae-mediated internalization. To add to this, size has a great effect on the subcellular distribution of particles; while larger QDs (diameter > 4 nm) are prominently situated in the cell's cytoplasm with limited entry into the nucleus, size <3 nm can predominantly internalize into the nucleus [70,71]. Therefore, the tiny QDs enter into the cell by pinocytosis (potentially caveolin-mediated endocytosis) due to their smaller size (2–6 nm) [63,72–74]. These reports corroborate with the present findings wherein GQDs not only demonstrated higher intensities in the cytoplasm and nucleus of the MCF-7 cells as compared to CQDs but also showed higher chances of entry into the nucleus attributing to their small size. Besides this, the positive zeta potential (12 mV) of GQDs led to electrostatic interaction with the negatively charged MCF-7 cell as well as its nuclear membrane [75], thereby resulting in greater internalization when compared to the negatively charged CQDs zeta potential (−16.7 mV) (S.Fig. 7) [68,76,77]. These results are in corroboration with Kumawat et al., who reported increased movement of GQDs into the nucleus of L929 fibroblast cells as a function of time. They suggested that the cell uptake via clathrin and caveolae-mediated endocytosis due to their smaller size and surface charge [63]. The detailed mechanism of cell internalization in our study is illustrated in (Fig. 4c), however, it needs further validation.

Next, we performed cell imaging using a 3D tumoroid model based on breast cancer cells in order to understand the cell internalization and fluorescent intensities of both QDs. It has been shown previously that 3D tumoroid model recapitulates the properties of solid tumor microenvironment similar to in vivo [78,79]. They further reflect some critical properties of solid tumors such as cell-cell and cell-ECM interaction as well as metabolic and drug gradients. In this regard, MCF-7 cell-derived tumoroids were developed by a previously established protocol in our laboratory and used for bioimaging. The tumoroids were incubated with CQDs and GQDs for 6 and 24 h and subjected to imaging using CLSM (Fig. 7 a). 3D tumoroids stained with DAPI were taken as the positive control. The results demonstrated multi-color fluorescence with different excitation wavelengths at 405, 485, and 543 nm. Interestingly, the GQDs exhibited greater fluorescence intensity than CQDs and DAPI; a similar trend was observed after the incubation of 3D tumoroids with QDs for 24 h (S.Fig. 9). The results corroborated with 2D culture and similar findings were observed in oral cancer cell line-based tumoroids as well (S.Fig. 10), thereby demonstrating the potential of GQDs in bioimaging.

Further, in vivo imaging system (IVIS®) was used to detect the CQDs and GQDs fluorescence signals in mice. CQDs and GQDs measurement was done using the excitation wavelength of 420 nm and emission wavelength successively at 500, 520, 540, 560, 580, and 600 nm. The results indicate that GQDs showed higher fluorescence compared to CQDs after intravenous administration in mice (Fig. 7b). The higher fluorescence intensity, as well as internalization, were explained well with the size and zeta potential in GQDs against CQDs. However, the size difference was relatively low between GQDs and CQDs, so we felt that the explanation was limited to establish the mechanism [80].

Certainly, the structural features of the carbon-based QDs have a definite role in the PL mechanism. The observed fluorescent intensity was quite high in GQDs as compared to CQDs and DAPI at different excitations in various cell lines (Fig. 5a).

In general, the PL effect of QDs has been explained with three different mechanisms: first, the quantum confinement effect, which is determined by the structure of the carbon core; second, the surface state, derived from hybridization of the carbon backbone and conjugated surface groups; and the third, the molecule state, which depends on the conjugated fluorescent molecules on the surface or interior of the QDs [24]. However, the underlying mechanism of differential PL in CQDs and GQDs cannot be explained fully, due to the large heterogeneity of the particles from the same synthesis or different procedures. The properties of QDs are highly variable and inconsistent from different experimental procedures thereby leading to the difficulty of defining the precise properties. Herein, we proposed a novel mechanism to understand the PL in CQDs and GQDs by considering the available DFT models with our experimental observations. As mentioned, currently, there is no standard experimental procedure available to explain the PL mechanism in QDs, but recently Sk et al. reported a theoretical model based on DFT to understand the influence of various factors to determine the properties precisely [26]. The team revealed that the PL in GQDs originated primarily from the quantum confinement of conjugated π-electrons in the sp 2 carbon network and can be sensitively tuned by various factors such as size, edge configuration, shape, conjugated functionalities, heteroatom doping, and defects.

Plausibly, the PL in CQDs is primarily from surface defects rather sp 2 clusters in the core, suggesting that the confinement effect has limited participation in fluorescence emission [7]. The low intensity of PL in CQDs can be well explained by their low QY (~6%) and it is due to disagreement between the emissive traps and non-radiated recombination.

Generally, a defect originates in a crystal by the deviation of an atom from its designated position in a periodically oriented crystal structure thereby impacting the properties of free carriers [81]. These imperfections in a crystal alter the localized electronic states from their carrier transport bands and provide photo-generated free carriers and get trapped. Thus, the trapped carriers have restrictions in their crystal mobility, resulting in additional energy states [82]. If the defective energy level resides just below or above the conduction band (CB) and valence band (VB) edges, respectively, it typically not restricts the emission intensities. Nevertheless, the defective state takes position between the band edges (energy gap) and can trap charges. The so-called shallow traps, which reside near to band edges (conduction/valence band edges) show slight redshift, while deep traps at the middle of the bandgap, typically hinder the detrapping and can induce non-radiative recombination. The illustration (Scheme 1 ) explains the lower PL intensity in CQDs from higher defects derived from lower crystallinity (XRD and NMR analysis) leading to deep trapping of the carriers and thereby resulting in reduced QY (depth trap emission). Further, high QY (44.1%) in GQDs can be explained with different mechanisms including crystallinity, surface edge shape, and confinement. Plausibly, the layered GQDs demonstrated high crystallinity similar to the graphitic structure with aromatic sp 2 domains surrounded by epoxy/hydroxyl sp 3 clusters [83]. The PL characteristics are derived from π states of sp 2 clusters. The PL of π → π* electronic transitions are influenced by the σ and σ* states of the sp 3 matrix towards strong confinement and recombination of excitation of sp 2 clusters further facilitating the fluorescence [84]. In addition to crystallinity, the surface-edge state of GQDs contains carbine due to its zigzag edge shape (Fig. 1c) and oxygen groups on the core. The zigzag edges are predominant for PL than the armchair due to the triplet ground state with a low bandgap [85]. The zigzag edge of GQDs's triple carbine induces a new bandgap, corresponding to the transition from the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) to σ and π orbital of the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) is responsible for PL GQDs instead of quantum confinement effect [86]. Furthermore, the quantum confinement effect plays a critical role in the PL of GQDs apart from crystallinity and surface states. The bandgap of the conjugated π-domains in perfect graphene core and fewer functional surface groups is considered the real PL center. The atomic features attained by QDs from their smaller sizes i.e., size less than the exciton Bohr radius (particle in a box). Typically size ranging from 1 to 10 nm of GQDs shows confinement effect and got intrinsic PL [87]. Therefore, it is concluded that the observed strong fluorescence intensity of GQDs in cell imaging is elucidated from various structural and molecular aspects (Scheme 1).

In conclusion, a facile one-pot hydrothermal route was successfully employed for the synthesis of CQDs and GQDs. The TEM micrographs confirm the spherical shape of CQDs with a mean diameter of ~5 nm and GQDs of 3 nm with an interlayer distance of 0.24 nm. AFM study further revealed GQDs with ~2 nm height, suggesting more than one layered graphene oxide stacks similar to crystalline graphite. Further, structural analysis was carried out using XRD analysis; results suggested amorphous and crystalline structure in CQDs and GQDs, respectively. Besides, XRD, the NMR spectra depicted more sp 2 clusters in GQDs as compared to CQDs, supported crystalline structure similar to graphite by claiming the presence of z-stacking. Furthermore, XPS and EDX analysis confirmed the presence of C, N, and O in CQDs and C, S, N, and O in GQDs. The fluorescence spectra of both QDs suggesting multi-color emission with variable excitations and reduced emission intensity were observed shifting towards higher wavelength excitations. Interestingly, GQDs showed a 7-fold higher QY as compared to CQDs with an almost similar lifetime. Time dependent stability studies of CQDs and GQDs were performed by various spectroscopic techniques and the QDs were found to be stable till 4 weeks. Fluorescence bioimaging in 2D cell culture, 3D tumoroids, and in vivo studies suggested greater fluorescent intensity and rapid cell internalization in GQDs when compared to CQDs. Stronger fluorescence in GQDs was possibly due to shallow emissions with concomitant quantum confinement effect, crystallinity, and surface edge effect. On the other hand, poor fluorescence in CQDs was due to competition between emission traps and non-radiated recombination of hole and electron. The observed rapid cell internalization in GQDs was attributed to possible electrostatic interactions between positive GQDs and negatively charged cell/nucleus membrane. Therefore, the GQDs can be a potential candidate for bioimaging and sensing amongst the carbon family. Further, they can be employed for theranostic applications by utilizing them as photodynamic agents and drug carriers.
